title: The Capital Rules: Basel, CET1 & Risk-Weighted Assets
quick_definition: Because banks are fragile and backed by the public, they're required to hold a minimum cushion of equity — set by the international Basel rules as a percentage of risk-weighted assets. The hardest, most loss-absorbing layer is CET1 — common equity, at least 4.5% of risk-weighted assets plus buffers — topped up by weaker layers and policed by stress tests.
explainer_covers: Why regulate this at all (Golin Ch. 9 → Admati): left alone, a subsidized, run-prone industry will hold too little cushion — so capital rules are the system's shock absorber.

★G287, assembled: the three layers (CET1 = common equity and retained earnings, minus deductions like goodwill and deferred-tax assets; AT1 = perpetual convertibles/CoCos; Tier 2 = subordinated debt); risk-weighted assets as the denominator — assets scaled by how risky they are, the cleverest and most game-able idea in the whole framework; and the headline: CET1 has to clear 4.5% plus buffers, which in practice binds the big banks around 10–13%.

The three pillars in one pass (Ramirez): minimum requirements, supervisory review, public disclosure — and where the U.S. stress tests (CCAR/DFAST) fit as often the real binding constraint for large banks, tighter than the ratio itself.

The critique to hold alongside the rules (Admati): risk-weights invite gaming, which is exactly why a plain leverage ratio (equity over total assets, unweighted) exists as a backstop — Basel III as an improvement, not a cure.

Why this matters even if you never analyze a bank: it's why banks can only pay out dividends and buybacks their capital position permits — the rules quietly govern what shareholders get (the hinge to Z4.1).
connects_to_raw: Z1.2 (capital's five meanings, regulatory one selected), Z4.1, Z4.2, `★G279` (securitization's original capital-arbitrage role), `macro-economics.z1.1` (the policy seat).

title: Runs, Panics & the Cycle: Why Finance Fails the Way It Does
quick_definition: A bank that funds itself with money people can demand back at any moment, while holding loans it can't quickly sell, is fragile by construction — and the bank run is the result: a self-fulfilling stampede for the exits. Deposit insurance is the cure, moral hazard is the cure's side effect, and too big to fail is the part no one has fully solved.
explainer_covers: ★G292, mechanically: because a bank turns short-term deposits into long-term loans, no bank — even a healthy one — can survive everyone withdrawing at once; runs feed on themselves (the It's a Wonderful Life logic); and why a liquidity panic and genuine insolvency blur together in the moment.

Deposit insurance as the circuit-breaker (FDIC, $250k): why it stops runs, and what it costs — insured depositors stop watching risk, so regulators and capital rules (Z5.1) have to watch instead; the post-2008 liquidity rules (LCR, NSFR) as the admission that capital alone doesn't stop a run.

Too big to fail: the implicit government backstop, the cheaper funding it hands the giants, and the surcharges and resolution/bail-in regimes (Z4.2) built to defuse it — with Admati's skepticism as the honest counterweight.

The sector and the economic cycle: bank losses cluster (★G255 — the credit cycle); banks lend freely at the top and slam the brakes at the bottom, amplifying downturns (★G238) — which is why financials are both the transmitter and the casualty of most crises (★G263's crisis canon lives in Macro; this node explains the sector's role in it).

The takeaway that ties the module together: markets permanently discount this fragility (that persistent below-book valuation), so any investment case in the sector is a joint bet on management, where we are in the cycle, and the rules holding.

connects_to.note: the crisis canon — the episodes this node's mechanics explain

connects_to_raw: `★G255`, `★G238`, `★G263`, `★G244` (the Fed as lender of last resort), Z1.2 (the leverage that makes runs lethal), Z5.1, Z4.2, `macro-economics.z5.9` (the crisis canon).
